evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-cis-problem-solving skill on this single message. Treat the scenario as fully specified and produce the initial structured problem-solving artifact directly (do not stop to interview the user step by step).

Problem as stated by the user: 'Our customer support team is drowning — tickets keep piling up and customers are angry.'
Context: a 40-person SaaS company; ticket volume up ~60% over six months; the same handful of issue types recur; the team size has not changed.

Produce a first-pass structured artifact that: (1) sharpens the vague complaint into a precise problem statement with a current-vs-desired gap; (2) bounds the problem (where/when it does and does NOT occur) using an Is/Is-Not style cut; (3) does root-cause analysis with a NAMED method (e.g. Five Whys or Fishbone) that drills past symptoms; and (4) only THEN generates a set of solution options spanning incremental and breakthrough approaches.
evals.expected_output: A diagnosis-first artifact that reframes the complaint into a precise problem statement, bounds it with an Is/Is-Not style analysis, applies a named root-cause method to reach underlying causes (not just symptoms), and only afterward proposes a diverse set of solution options.
expectations: The response transforms the vague complaint into a precise problem statement that articulates the gap between the current state and a desired state, rather than treating 'team is drowning' as the problem to solve directly.
It bounds the problem with an Is/Is-Not style analysis (where/when/who the problem does and does not affect), surfacing patterns from those boundaries.

It performs root-cause analysis using at least one explicitly NAMED method (such as Five Whys, Fishbone/Ishikawa, or Systems Thinking) and reaches underlying causes rather than stopping at surface symptoms.

Solution generation comes AFTER diagnosis and offers multiple options spanning incremental and more transformative approaches — the response does not jump straight to 'hire more agents' as the sole fix before any root-cause work.

evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-cis-problem-solving skill on this single message; produce the initial structured artifact directly without an interactive back-and-forth.

Problem: a mid-size warehouse has rising order-fulfillment errors (wrong items shipped). Errors spike on high-volume days and during shift handovers. Returns and re-ships are eating margin.

Produce a structured problem-solving artifact: refine the problem statement and success criteria, run a named root-cause analysis, generate at least 8 solution options using named generation methods, then evaluate the options against explicit criteria using a named evaluation method (e.g. a decision matrix) and recommend a direction with its rationale and remaining risks.
evals.expected_output: An artifact with a refined problem statement and success criteria, a named root-cause analysis tied to the high-volume / shift-handover signals, 8+ solution options from named methods, and a named evaluation method (e.g. decision matrix) producing a reasoned recommendation with residual risks.
expectations: The response produces a refined problem statement plus explicit success criteria for what 'solved' looks like.
It applies a NAMED root-cause method and connects the analysis to the given signals (errors spiking on high-volume days and at shift handovers) rather than ignoring those clues.
It generates at least 8 distinct solution options, ideally noting the generation method(s) used, spanning a mix of process, tooling, and human/organizational fixes.

It evaluates options against explicit criteria using a NAMED evaluation method (such as a decision matrix, cost-benefit, or risk matrix) and recommends a direction with clear rationale and remaining risks — rather than asserting one fix with no comparison.

evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-cis-problem-solving skill on this single message; produce the initial structured artifact directly.

Problem as stated: 'Our mobile app's onboarding is bad — new users drop off, so we need a redesign of the onboarding screens.'
Context: 70% of new signups never complete account setup; drop-off is concentrated at the email-verification and permissions-request steps.

IMPORTANT: A correct response applies the skill's diagnose-before-solve method — it must NOT accept the user's pre-baked solution ('redesign the onboarding screens') as the problem and jump straight to UI suggestions. It must reframe the actual problem, bound it, and reach root causes with a named method BEFORE proposing solutions.
evals.expected_output: A diagnosis-first artifact that resists the user's pre-supplied solution, reframes the real problem (signup completion / drop-off at specific steps), bounds it, applies a named root-cause method, and only then proposes options. Jumping straight to 'redesign the screens' UI tips with no root-cause work is the failure this case targets.
expectations: The response explicitly resists treating the user's pre-baked solution ('redesign the onboarding screens') as the problem, and instead reframes the underlying problem (e.g. low signup-completion rate, drop-off concentrated at email-verification and permissions steps).
It bounds the problem and applies at least one NAMED root-cause method to investigate WHY users drop off at those specific steps before proposing fixes.
Any solutions appear only after diagnosis and address the diagnosed root causes, spanning more than just visual/screen redesign.

HONESTY CONTROL: a response that accepts 'redesign the onboarding screens' at face value and jumps straight to UI/screen suggestions with no problem reframing and no named root-cause analysis FAILS this expectation.
